title: Cooking with Spices
content:
SPICES

Purchase from reputable sources; replenish ground spices annually; date jars. Store tightly covered, cool, dark. Put whole spices in cloth bag or tea ball for easy removal. Do not overboil pepper or caraway (becomes bitter). Do not use high heat for paprika or curry (scorch).

Allspice: Soup to nuts; alone or combined. Contains cinnamon, clove, nutmeg, juniper flavors.

Cardamom: Powder seeds as needed. With cinnamon, cloves; in coffee. Small type used whole in barbecue sauces.

Cinnamon/Cassia: True cinnamon (Ceylon) mild and sweet. Most "cinnamon" is cassia (flat scroll, slightly bitter). Use stick form in hot chocolate, mulled wine, fruit compotes, pickles.

Cloves: Strong; remove heads for milder flavor. Remove whole cloves before serving. With ham, curries, stewed fruits, marmalades, chutneys, pickles, marinades, onions, meats.

Curry: Highly seasoned sauce best made fresh from spices, onion, garlic, fruits, vegetables. Develop flavor in olive oil or ghee; cook slowly hours before adding to main food. Range from mild Indonesian to fierce Madras.

Ginger: Fresh root with smooth skin, uniformly buff. Keep dry or will sprout. Refrigerated in plastic bag 3 weeks, or in sherry. Slice, grate, or mash for use. Dried: steep cubes in marinade or cold water; use liquid for seasoning. Crystallized/candied for desserts.

Nutmeg/Mace: From same fruit. Mace is lacy outer covering; nutmeg is inner kernel. Grate fresh for full flavor. In baked items, spinach, veal, French toast, eggnog. 1 whole nutmeg = 2-3 teaspoons ground.

Pepper: White from ripe berries (shell buffed); black from underripe fermented berries. White better for light sauces and canned meats; black for general use. Best freshly ground. Crush peppercorns for poivrade dishes; add last few minutes.

Saffron: Stigmas of autumn crocus. Golden orange color and distinctive flavor. Use sparingly—¼ teaspoon in 2 tablespoons hot liquid colors 5-6 cups flour. For seasoning 6-8 servings sauce: ⅛-¼ teaspoon in 2 tablespoons hot liquid or white wine.

Vanilla: Bean fermented and cured 6 months. Extract: macerated in 35% alcohol. Add to cooling foods to retain flavor. Keep beans in brandy; use flavored brandy as seasoning. 1 inch scraped bean = 1 teaspoon extract. Beware synthetic vanillas (ruin frozen dishes).
